metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Workplace Mathematics 12 is the capstone course of the Workplace Mathematics pathway, consolidating and extending practical mathematical skills for work, citizenship, and everyday life. Students apply proportional reasoning, measurement, personal finance, data analysis, and problem-solving in contexts relevant to trades, small business, and independent living. This course satisfies the Grade 12 mathematics requirement for graduation and supports direct-to-workforce and apprenticeship pathways.
metadata.general_description: The capstone Workplace Mathematics course applying practical proportional reasoning, measurement, personal finance, data analysis, and problem-solving to authentic workplace, trades, and everyday contexts — completing the graduation requirement for the applied mathematics pathway.

big_ideas.statement: Proportional reasoning is used to make sense of multiplicative relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Proportional reasoning — comparing relative rather than absolute quantities — is foundational to trades, health care, design, cooking, and countless other workplace applications.

big_ideas.statement: Mathematics informs financial decision making throughout life.
big_ideas.elaboration: Understanding mortgages, investments, insurance, taxation, and retirement planning empowers individuals to make sound long-term financial decisions.

big_ideas.statement: 3D objects can be examined mathematically by measuring and calculating properties.
big_ideas.elaboration: Technical trades and design fields require the ability to calculate volumes, surface areas, and dimensions of complex three-dimensional objects from technical drawings and specifications.

big_ideas.statement: Representing and analyzing data allows us to notice and wonder about relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Interpreting and critically evaluating data representations is essential for making evidence-based decisions in the workplace and as an informed citizen.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies; explore and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and tools; estimate reasonably; model with mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively with curiosity.
i_can_statements: I can develop and apply thinking strategies to solve practical and workplace mathematical problems.
I can model real-world financial, measurement, and statistical situations with mathematics.
I can estimate reasonably and judge whether results make sense in context.
I can approach unfamiliar problems creatively using reasoning and available tools.

competencies.raw: Develop and apply conceptual understanding through inquiry and problem solving; visualize mathematical concepts; apply flexible and strategic approaches; solve with persistence; engage in place-based problem-solving.
i_can_statements: I can apply mathematical understanding to solve complex financial and measurement problems.
I can visualize 3D objects and interpret technical drawings.
I can select and apply multiple strategies — proportional reasoning, formulas, technology — to solve practical problems.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas in many ways; represent ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary; take risks in discourse.
i_can_statements: I can clearly explain and justify mathematical reasoning in practical and workplace contexts.
I can represent financial and measurement solutions using tables, diagrams, graphs, and written explanations.
I can communicate confidently using appropriate mathematical vocabulary.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect concepts with each other and personal interests; use mistakes as learning opportunities; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, knowledge, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my mathematical approaches and identify improvements.
I can connect mathematics to career planning, small business, and independent living.
I can incorporate First Peoples knowledge and practices, including traditional measurement and resource management, into my thinking.

areas.name: Personal Finance and Investment
areas.raw: Personal finance: mortgages, investments, insurance, taxes, and retirement.
topics: Mortgages: down payment, amortization period, monthly payments, total interest paid
Investment growth: compound interest and investment accounts over time
Retirement planning: overview of RRSPs, pensions, and long-term saving
Income taxes: progressive tax rates, deductions, credits, and completing a basic return
Insurance: home, vehicle, life — understanding coverage and cost-benefit
Credit: credit cards, credit scores, and responsible debt management

areas.name: Measurement and 3D Applications
areas.raw: Measurement: compound 3D objects, conversions, and technical applications.
topics: Composite 3D objects: calculating surface area and volume of compound shapes
Precision and accuracy: significant figures, tolerances, and measurement error
Metric and imperial conversions: practical applications in trades
Reading technical drawings: floor plans, blueprints, and construction diagrams
Material estimation: calculating quantities for construction, landscaping, and trades projects

areas.name: Proportional and Algebraic Reasoning
areas.raw: Proportional reasoning; rates; applied problem solving.
topics: Complex rate problems: unit rates, combined rates, and rate comparisons
Proportional problem solving in health care: dosage calculations
Business mathematics: markup, markdown, profit margin, and break-even analysis
Scale: applying scale factors in blueprints, maps, and technical drawings

areas.name: Data Analysis
areas.raw: Representing and analyzing data in workplace and social contexts.
topics: Statistical analysis: interpreting mean, median, mode, range, and standard deviation
Data visualization: creating and critically evaluating graphs and charts
Making decisions from data: identifying trends and drawing conclusions
Probability in workplace contexts: quality control, reliability, and risk
